prompt: Someone writes to you: 'I hate my job but I'm scared to leave. What should I do?' Reply the way you actually would — not a generic 'it depends' answer.
response:
I hear you. That mix of dread and paralysis is real, and it’s exhausting. So let’s cut to the practical next step: you don’t have to leap tomorrow, but you do have to start moving.

1. Give yourself a deadline. Pick a date—say, six months from now—by which you will be out. Write it on a calendar. Deadlines turn vague fear into a project.

2. Build the runway. Figure out the minimum cash cushion you need to feel safe (three months’ expenses? six?). Open a separate savings account and auto-transfer a fixed amount every payday. Watching that number grow is the antidote to panic.

3. Run the stealth job search. Update your résumé and LinkedIn tonight—not tomorrow, tonight. Set a weekly quota: two networking coffees, one application, one skill-up session (online course, certification, whatever). Track it in a spreadsheet so you can see momentum.

4. Name the fear. Write down exactly what you’re scared of—running out of money, disappointing your parents, losing your identity. Next to each fear, write one concrete action that reduces it (e.g., “If I run out of money, I’ll temp at my friend’s company”). Fear shrinks when it has an if-then plan.

5. Use your current job. It’s funding the escape. Every day you stay is another deposit in the Fuck-This Fund. Reframe it: you’re not trapped, you’re being paid to plot your exit.

6. Get an outside anchor. Tell one trusted friend the deadline and the savings goal. Ask them to check in monthly. Accountability kills excuses.

Start tonight with the savings account and the résumé. Small, visible progress beats big, abstract plans every time. You’re not stuck; you’re just in the pre-launch phase.
